retrieved_docs.title: Coping Mechanisms -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Coping influences patients' compliance to therapy and the course of the disease by lifestyle changes. In disorders where non-medicinal treatment plays a role in the progression, coping mechanisms are important in determining the severity of such conditions. Coping styles may be helpful in patients' educational programs or psychotherapy, and paying attention to them could contribute to the prevention of sequelae. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Assessment and Treatment of Psychiatric Patients -- Issues of Concern -- Varying Backgrounds
retrieved_docs.content: Some of the psychopharmacological considerations for patients of varying backgrounds are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Dependent Personality Disorder -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Research on the treatment and management of dependent personality disorder remains limited. Most current knowledge is based on older, small sample-size investigations, case reports, or case series. There are also significant limitations to the existing models for describing personality disorders. The cluster system is the most commonly used model based on the different editions of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders . The uniqueness of each individual's personality also poses challenges for diagnosing and researching specific personality disorders. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dependent Personality Disorder -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of dependent personality disorder depends on developing and maintaining a therapeutic alliance with the patient in a safe and supportive environment. Patients are encouraged to express the symptoms they wish to have addressed and communicate any psychosocial stressors that a treatment team can help alleviate. Rather than primarily focusing on changing the patient's worldview, clinicians should aim to understand and address the specific concerns and challenges that the patient is facing. This approach is particularly relevant when the patient is not in acute distress or crisis. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Individual resilience and coping skills: The patient's resilience and coping skills can influence the prognosis. Those who develop effective coping strategies and have a higher level of resilience may have a more favorable prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Adlerian Therapy -- Function -- Lifestyle
retrieved_docs.content: Lifestyle or style of life, the Adlerian terminology for personality, is a personal metanarrative that involves an individual's particular beliefs, purposes, and principles for dealing with the adversities of living. This life plan is distinctive for each person and emerges early in life, approximately when the individual reaches 6 years of age. Lifestyle consists of our comprehension of events rather than the events themselves, profoundly influencing our personalities. This notion includes self-concept, self-ideals, views of the world, and ethical convictions. Later, this cognitive orientation is molded within the initial social setting of the family constellation. The family constellation consists of family atmosphere, values, and gender lines. [7] These aspects influence an individual's lifestyle and determine how we act, think, perceive, or live. We select methods for enduring life challenges and cues from developing lifestyles. According to the Adlerian theory, humans are proactive and creative in developing their lifestyle, functioning as playwrights and actors while constructing their personalities within a socially embedded context. Adler outlined 4 basic lifestyles: the socially useful, the ruling type, the getting type, and the avoiding type. [8] The "socially useful" is also described as a responsible lifestyle. This category includes individuals who are the healthiest, most complete, and most cheerful typology. They possess a strong sense of social responsibility and strive daily to improve themselves, overcome their worries, build resilience, and eliminate greed. Socially useful individuals tend to be mature, optimistic, well-adjusted, polite, and respectful of others. They do not aim for superiority but to solve adversities in ways that are cognizant of others. [9] The "ruling type" is also described as a selfish lifestyle. Individuals with limited social interests attempt to demonstrate authority and seek dominance over others. These people tend to be confrontational and possess harsh energy that subjugates anyone who gets in their way. They adopt an antisocial approach to society. The most energetic of these are intimidators and sadists. Meanwhile, less active individuals turn their power inward and hurt themselves, such as individuals with substance use disorders and suicidal ideation. [9] The "getting type" is also known as a dependent lifestyle. These individuals conduct their existence in one direction: obtaining reinforcements from others. They usually want to receive everything from others without any effort or struggle. They have low social interests and non-reciprocity. The main explanation for their behavior is that they have not overcome their feelings of inferiority. Consequently, they develop relationships of dependence and absolute submission. [9] The "avoiding type" is also recognized as an escapist lifestyle. These people try to dodge duties, evade responsibilities, and cannot maintain relationships. They display childish behaviors, pursue an easy life, and experience immediate satisfaction. They tend to have the lowest energy and prefer unrealistic daydreaming and being fanciful instead of facing reality. Additionally, individuals of this type demonstrate little social contact because of fear of rejection, evading life in general and others in particular. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Coping Mechanisms -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Patients using maladaptive coping mechanisms are more likely to engage in health-risk behaviors than those with appropriate mechanisms. They are also more non-adherent and more likely to use cigarettes or alcohol. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Avoidant Personality Disorder -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Experts in personality disorders have suggested switching to a dimensional model of personality rather than a cluster model. The dimensional models proposed generally describe temperament, utilization of defense mechanisms, and identifying pathological personality traits. [29] Although the DSM-5 did not incorporate these recommendations due to the sudden radical change it would imply for clinical use, the paradigm will likely shift in the coming decades as further research solidifies in congruence with evolving clinical guidelines. This is particularly evident as the DSM-5-TR incorporated this research into publication under the "emerging measures and models" section. Notably, in this section of the DSM-5-TR, some of the "cluster" model personality disorders have been removed, but AVPD remains a named personality disorder. The alternative dimensional model of AVPD identifies the internalization of distress, high negative affectivity, behavioral inhibition and avoidance, and low levels of extraversion as salient diagnostic features. [30] [31]

Dimensional models generally describe temperament, utilize defense mechanisms, and identify pathological personality traits. This potential shift is particularly evident as the DSM-5-TR has incorporated this research under emerging measures and models.

retrieved_docs.title: Dependent Personality Disorder -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Experts in personality disorders have suggested switching to a dimensional model of personality rather than a cluster model. The proposed dimensional models are variously based on temperament, utilization of defense mechanisms, level of functioning, and pathological personality traits. [44] The DSM-5-TR includes an "Alternative DSM-5 Model for Personality Disorders," which no longer includes dependent personality disorder. [45] [46] Overlooked areas for research on dependent personality disorder are biological studies, cultural considerations, cognitive processes, use of community samples, and treatment. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Avoidant Personality Disorder -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of AVPD is impingent upon developing and maintaining therapeutic rapport. Therapists should offer reassurance that the environment is safe and supportive. Patients are encouraged to express the symptoms they wish to have addressed and communicate any psychosocial stressors that a treatment team can help alleviate. Rather than primarily focusing on changing the patient's worldview, clinicians should aim to understand and address the specific concerns and challenges that the patient is facing. This approach is particularly relevant when the patient is not in acute distress or crisis when alone. [3] Further, patients are encouraged to utilize support networks through their remaining social relationships and expand on these as they develop comfort and confidence. Involving the patient's family is another way of monitoring for decompensation and providing education on how to provide stable social factors for the patient. [36] Utilizing standardized assessments for quality of life may reveal ways to optimize the ability to function in significant areas of life for an individual with AVPD.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Communication In Substance Use Disorders -- Clinical Significance -- Effect on Patient Satisfaction
retrieved_docs.content: Maintaining a positive attitude and believing that patients can recover is an essential motivator for the provider. If patients sense that the clinician is offering positive social support, they may be less apt to give up on themselves. It is important to realize that patients seek assistance in some of the most vulnerable and sensitive moments in their lives. Patients may have experienced some form of socioeconomic hardship and have resorted to using substances as a coping mechanism. Furthermore, explaining a diagnosis transparently gives patients the sense that the disease is treatable and offers closure and peace of mind.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizoid Personality Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This disorder can adversely affect multiple aspects of life, underscoring the need for early intervention and interdisciplinary care. Diagnosis of schizoid personality disorder is based on criteria outlined in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders , 5th ed., Text Revision ( DSM-5-TR ). Individuals with this disorder often present for treatment due to external pressures from family or maladaptive behaviors affecting others rather than personal distress. Management primarily focuses on reducing interpersonal conflict and stabilizing socioeconomic conditions rather than aiming for remission of the disorder. This activity demonstrates the evaluation and management of schizoid personality disorder and highlights the role of the interdisciplinary healthcare team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2287
retrieved_docs.content: Major depressive disorder with a recurrent episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode in full remission, atypical depression (اكتئاب أوبِيّ), and mood lability (التقلقل الوجداني) are all classified as members of the schizophrenia and psychotic disorders SMQ (الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)). The SMQ itself is categorized under the broader construct of blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). This hierarchical organization indicates that these depressive conditions are considered within the spectrum of schizophrenia‑related and psychotic disorders, and that the entire group is associated with a reduction in emotional expressivity. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the overlap between mood disturbances and psychotic features, and underscores the importance of assessing affective flattening when evaluating patients with these diagnoses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: integrating various organic‑based mood and transient disturbances that do not fit more specific categories. This structured framework aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 956
retrieved_docs.content: Mental symptom is a broad clinical concept that includes diverse manifestations such as personality problems, loss of pleasure (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior. These mental symptoms are linked to psychotic conditions; specifically, they have a broader relationship with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة). Transient organic psychotic states are a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which itself is broadly related to schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). The overarching category of psychosis (الذهان) therefore encompasses transient organic psychotic states, situating them within the spectrum of organic psychosis and its association with schizophrenic delusional phenomena. This integrated view highlights how specific mental symptoms connect to distinct forms of organic psychosis and their broader psychotic correlates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 367
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol and drug rehabilitation/detoxification is linked to متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول (alcohol abstinence syndrome), which in turn includes الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) as a component of its clinical picture. الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) comprises a broad spectrum of psychotic and affective conditions. It contains ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood schizophrenia, active or current state), reflecting that disorders usually diagnosed in infancy have a broader relationship with early‑onset psychotic presentations. Within the SMQ group are several depressive entities: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (obligatory depression), and التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Additionally, الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) is associated with عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect), indicating affective flattening as a characteristic feature of this syndrome cluster. Together, these relationships illustrate how substance‑use treatment, early‑life psychotic disorders, various depressive states, mood instability, and blunted affect are interconnected within the framework of the SMQ and alcohol abstinence syndrome, highlighting important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 960
retrieved_docs.content: Mental symptom is broadly associated with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة), which are classified as a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي). Organic psychosis, in turn, has a broader relationship with schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). Within the category of mental symptom, specific manifestations include self‑destructive behavior (السلوك التدميري الذاتي), loss of pleasure or anhedonia (فقد المتعة), and personality‑related problems (probleem; persoonlijkheid). Recognizing how these individual symptoms relate to underlying organic psychotic processes and broader psychotic phenomena such as schizophrenic delusions supports more comprehensive assessment and targeted patient care.

These components illustrate that mental symptom can involve personality‑related problems, behaviors that are harmful to oneself, and a diminished ability to experience enjoyment, all of which are important considerations in assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. This relationship indicates that brief, organic‑based psychotic episodes are considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders, highlighting an overlap between anxiety symptomatology and short‑lasting psychotic states. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad category of mental health conditions that encompasses more specific disorders such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation‑anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال), each of which is a narrower subtype within the overall class of psychological disorders. Both the general category of psychological disorder and its child conditions—depression and separation‑anxiety disorder—are identified as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that strategies aimed at preventing onset, reducing severity, and managing symptoms are applicable across the spectrum of these mental health conditions. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of applying preventive and control interventions at both the overarching disorder level and the individual disorder level to improve patient outcomes.

Recognizing that حركات متكررة نمطية and تبلد المشاعر can be clinical expressions of ضمور المخ المحدد, and that ذُهان عضوي is associated with هوس شَبَقِيّ, helps clinicians integrate neurological and psychiatric findings for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: The category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy” has a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active). This specific psychotic condition is a member of the larger classification الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) (psychosis and psychotic disorders). Within the الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) group, several mood‑related diagnoses are also listed as members: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (atypical depression), and the symptom التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Moreover, the entire الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) category is itself a member of عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect). This network of relationships illustrates the overlap between early‑onset psychotic conditions, broader psychotic disorder classifications, depressive mood disorders, and affective symptoms such as blunted affect, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: disorder, representing an inverse‑ISA behavioral pattern. Transient and continuous disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance (الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه, الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره) are linked to both non‑infectious encephalitis and the depressive syndrome, sharing features such as early excessive daytime sleepiness, hyperkinetic activity and apathy. Adolescent mental disorder is treated as a developmental subtype of psychological disorder characterized by pathological sexual development, solitary‑aggressive behavior, personality problems (probleem; persoonlijkheid), anhedonia (فقد المتعة) and self‑destructive behavior, and it commonly co‑occurs with neurodevelopmental conditions such as attention‑deficit disorder. Early‑onset psychotic conditions are related to unspecified childhood psychosis in an active state (ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة) and are incorporated within the broader psychosis and psychotic disorders
